documents.title: Brandeis University public AI hub or guidance

ai_strategy.summary: Brandeis University's public AI posture is visible through governance guidance, teaching resources, research activity, and enterprise/tool support. The evidence points to a pragmatic R1 approach: encourage AI literacy and research while constraining sensitive data and academic-integrity risks.

key_provisions: Use AI in ways consistent with academic integrity and existing university policy.
Protect sensitive, confidential, student, research, or regulated data when using AI tools.
Faculty or academic units retain responsibility for clear course-level expectations where applicable.
Human review and accountability remain necessary for AI-assisted work.
ai_policy.summary: Brandeis University has public AI guidance or policy-adjacent materials. The cited guidance addresses responsible use, data protection, ethics, and course or work-context expectations.

initiatives.title: Artificial Intelligence Steering Council AI governance and guidance
initiatives.description: Brandeis University publishes AI guidance or governance resources that frame responsible use, academic integrity, privacy, and institutional oversight. The cited materials make AI a university-level issue rather than only a classroom technology topic.
initiatives.significance: This is a material public signal of how an R1 university is translating AI from discussion into institutional practice.

initiatives.policy_implications: The public guidance creates an institutional baseline for course, research, and administrative AI use.

initiatives.title: GenAI-aware oral and process assessment guidance
initiatives.description: Brandeis CTL guidance recommends oral assessments, process-focused assignments, lower-stakes work, in-class writing, and course-specific AI expectations as responses to generative AI.
initiatives.significance: The guidance is a concrete liberal-arts assessment redesign response to GenAI rather than a generic academic-integrity warning.

computing_infrastructure.summary: Brandeis documents a centrally administered HPCC for research computing and computational studies, composed of compute and GPU servers owned by Division of Science labs. Separate public pages describe a CS Slurm GPU cluster and a Digital Scholarship Lab high-performance workstation with an RTX 6000; a consolidated GPU count was not public.

equity_and_access_initiatives.title: Brandeis University responsible AI and access signal
equity_and_access_initiatives.description: Cited public materials connect AI adoption to privacy, bias, academic integrity, access, community benefit, or responsible human use.

regulatory_context.summary: Massachusetts public AI activity is relevant context; this profile treats campus policy and institutional evidence as the primary governance layer.

narrative_summary: Brandeis University presents an AI posture typical of an active R1 institution: public guidance for generative AI, visible teaching resources, research activity, and a growing set of supported tools or infrastructure. The most mature signals are governance and responsible-use guidance, teaching and syllabus support, and research capacity through Core Machine Learning Lab and Robotics Lab. Computer Science AI and machine learning coursework gives the institution a workforce-development or curriculum signal, while Google Gemini, Microsoft Copilot Chat, Zoom AI shows that AI is also being handled as an enterprise technology and data-governance issue. Public sources also show attention to responsible use, access, bias, privacy, or community impact, though some operational details remain incomplete. In particular, current public pages do not consistently disclose named executive ownership, all procurement decisions, full user counts, or complete dollar amounts. This profile therefore treats the institution as operational and growing rather than fully transformative: AI is embedded across policy, pedagogy, research, and tools, but not every part of the campus AI operating model is publicly documented.
